perturbation.original_text: The Agreement is governed by and shall be construed in accordance with the laws of England and the parties submit to the nonexclusive jurisdiction of the English Courts.
perturbation.changed_text: The Agreement is governed by and shall be construed in accordance with the laws of England. Notices shall be delivered personally, or by prepaid first class mail, or transmitted by email to the parties at such address or number as the parties shall specify from time to time.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the governing law to be near the notices section removes the explicit jurisdiction clause from its intended location. This affects enforceability, because the section defining which courts have authority is crucial.
perturbation.law_explanation: This act codifies the rules for determining the applicable law in contract disputes. Removing the clear jurisdiction statement makes it harder to quickly ascertain which law governs the contract, potentially leading to disputes about jurisdiction and applicable law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Page Not Found | GovInfo Skip to main content Page Not Found Help Error occurred. The page you requested cannot be found. Please report this error to askGPO. Provide the following information to help us resolve this problem: the URL of the page you were trying to access, the steps you followed to produce the error, specific search or browse terms, and/or a screenshot of the page where the error occurred. Thank you for your patience. Homepage Search Tips Skip back to top
perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly defines the jurisdiction (English Courts), whereas the changed contract removes this explicit jurisdiction clause, relocating the governing law clause and adding a notices section. The perturbation explanation indicates this affects enforceability. The LLM-generated law explanation states that removing the jurisdiction statement makes it harder to ascertain which law governs the contract. The absence of a clear jurisdiction statement makes it more challenging to determine the applicable law, potentially leading to disputes and undermining the contract's enforceability. Since the scraped law snippets are not usable, the decision is based on the other elements provided, in particular the law explanation, the perturbation explanation and the definitions for structural flaws legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All additions or modifications to this Agreement must be made in writing and must be signed by both parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement does not create any agency or partnership relationship.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the writing requirement for all additions and modifications to the contract affects enforceability, as oral agreements are more difficult to prove and enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 2 requires contracts for the sale or disposition of interests in land to be in writing. While this NDA may not directly involve land, removing the general 'in writing' requirement could lead to future modifications concerning land interests being unenforceable if not in writing, contradicting this Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Fraud Act 2006 | The Crown Prosecution Service Skip to main content Accessibility controls Contrast Switch to colour theme Switch to blue theme Switch to high visibility theme Switch to soft theme Open Site Search Search for Search for Main content area Fraud Act 2006 Updated: 16 July 2020; 11 March 2024 | Legal Guidance , Fraud and economic crime Introduction Charging Practice Overlap with theft and other offences Drafting the charge Use of cheques and cheque or credit cards The borderline between criminal and civil liability Arguments over Ownership of Property Loss to a third party The Offences Fraud by false representation (Section 2) Fraud by failing to disclose information (Section 3) Drafting the charge Fraud by abuse of position (Section 4) Drafting the charge Possession of articles for use in fraud (Section 6) Making or supplying articles for use in frauds (Section 7) Participation by sole trader in fraudulent business (Section 9) Charging practice Obtaining services dishonestly (Section 11) Charging practice Liability of company officers for offences by company (Section 12) The Elements of the Offences False representation Untrue or misleading Dishonesty Gain or loss Failure to disclose information Establishing a legal duty Abuse of a position Possession or control Article Services Obtains for himself or another Evidence of spouse/partner (Section 3) Penalty Introduction The Fraud Act 2006 (the Act) came into force on 15 January 2007 and applies in England, Wales and Northern Ireland. The Act repealed the following offences: Theft Act 1968 Section15 (obtaining property by deception); Section15A (obtaining a money transfer by deception); Section 16 (obtaining a pecuniary advantage by deception); Section 20(2) (procuring the execution of a valuable security by deception); Reference to "cheat" in Section 25 (going equipped). Theft Act 1978 Section 1 (obtaining services by deception); Section 2 (evasion of liability by deception). These offences continue to apply for any offences committed before 15 January 2007. When it is uncertain when a relevant event occurred and it may have happened before, on or after 15 January 2007 prosecutors should request that police obtain as much information as possible to assist in identifying the date on which any relevant events occurred. In cases when the uncertainty cannot be resolved it is proper practice to put alternative counts on the indictment under the 2006 Act and the previous legislation. R v Bellman [1989] AC 836 held that mutually exclusive counts on a single indictment can be left to the jury where there is a prima facie case on both. Charging P
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that all modifications must be in writing and signed by both parties. The changed text removes this requirement entirely. The Law Explanation suggests this could lead to future unenforceable modifications, specifically those concerning land interests. Although the provided law snippet references the Fraud Act 2006, the Perturbation Explanation asserts that removing the 'in writing' requirement for contract modifications affects enforceability, because oral agreements are difficult to prove. This aligns with the Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction definition, as the change affects the contract's enforceability, which is a structural flaw.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement does not create any agency or partnership relationship. This Agreement will not be assignable or transferable by either party.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement does not create any agency or partnership relationship.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the prohibition on assignment or transfer affects enforceability, because assignment could radically alter the responsibilities and liabilities of the parties.
perturbation.law_explanation: This act allows third parties to enforce terms of a contract if it expressly provides for it, or purports to confer a benefit on them. Removing the clause preventing assignment weakens the original parties' control over who can enforce the contract against them, potentially allowing unintended third parties to acquire rights.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text prohibited assignment or transfer by either party. The changed text removes this prohibition. The Perturbation Explanation states that removing the prohibition on assignment or transfer affects enforceability, because assignment could radically alter the responsibilities and liabilities of the parties. The Law Explanation states that removing the clause preventing assignment weakens the original parties' control over who can enforce the contract against them, potentially allowing unintended third parties to acquire rights. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses third-party rights, confirming the relevance of assignment to enforceability. The change contradicts the original intent by allowing assignment, which could introduce unintended third-party enforcers and alter the original responsibilities and liabilities, undermining the enforceability for the original parties and aligns with the structural flaws legal contradiction definition.
